name: JL Ticker OTF
classification_notes: A modular, experimental typeface built on clock-position logic with shape-shifting cuts; firmly display in nature, intended for layering and typographic play rather than continuous text.
summary: Four OTF cuts of a clock-inspired modular typeface by Karel Martens and Jungmyung Lee, each named after a clock position and designed for layering, overprinting and typographic experimentation.
description: Each of the four cuts — Four, Six, Eight and Nine — corresponds to a clock position, producing distinct width and stroke variations through manual expansion, compression and selective removal of external bars. The cuts are designed to be combined, layered and overprinted rather than used as conventional text typefaces, with colour experimentation central to the concept. Described by the foundry as a digital toy rather than a functionally complete type system, the work sits closer to typographic apparatus than type family.
foundry_blurb: The foundry describes JL Ticker OTF as four separate cuts alongside the variable version, named after clock positions: Four, Six, Eight and Nine. Through shape-shifting — expanding or compressing widths, removing external bars, layering, colour experimentation and overprinting — it offers endless possibilities for dynamic typographic compositions. Ticker is presented not as a functionally complete typeface but as a spirit: an open, elementary modular playground, a digital toy for colouring and constructing language.

name: JL Ticker
classification_notes: A geometric, modular type system built from elementary shapes with variable behaviour across three distinct styles; its primary purpose is pattern-making and colour-layered composition rather than conventional text setting, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A collaborative modular type system by Karel Martens and Jungmyung Lee built around shape-shifting glyphs that animate from vertical to diagonal to rotating through a variable font axis.
description: The third typeface collaboration between Karel Martens and Jungmyung Lee, Ticker is an elementary modular system in which letterforms shift across three behavioural modes: vertical to horizontal, diagonal to reverse, and clock-like rotation. Each behaviour is a distinct style, with the variable font allowing continuous transition between states via a slider. Designed for colour layering, overprinting and pattern-making, it is conceived explicitly as a playground rather than a workhorse text face.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee describe Ticker as an open, elementary modular playground and digital toy for colouring and constructing language. Inspired by Martens's mathematical precision and playful approach to colour, shape and movement, it offers three distinct variable behaviours and supports fully customised designs ranging from micro to macro, monochrome to vibrantly colourful.

name: JL Belligerent Times
classification_notes: Derived from Times New Roman, itself a transitional serif commissioned for The Times of London in 1931; Belligerent Times maintains the transitional serif structure while recontextualising it conceptually.
summary: A conceptually driven Times New Roman derivative by Jungmyung Lee that reframes the familiar typeface as a statement about the political and historical conditions of the present.
description: Rather than treating Times New Roman purely as a typographic form to be refined, Belligerent Times begins with the word embedded in its name, reading 'times' as historical moment and social circumstance rather than merely newspaper heritage. Released as a single Semibold weight in OTF, WOFF and WOFF2 formats, it covers Latin script across Western European, Central European and South Eastern European character sets. The conceptual framing, paired with specimen texts by Chantal Akerman and Joan Didion, positions it squarely in editorial and artistic contexts where typographic familiarity is a point of departure rather than an end.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee Type Foundry describe Belligerent Times as initiated by an interest in the word 'Times' itself, tracing its lineage from the 1785 founding of The Daily Universal Register through to Stanley Morison and Victor Lardent's 1931 commission for The Times of London. Rather than offering another formal reinterpretation of Times New Roman, J-LTF frame the typeface as a reflection on 'times' as historical moments, social conditions and political circumstances, transforming a familiar typographic reference into a contemporary statement.

name: JL Limpet Granite
classification_notes: Reinterpreted from Plantin, a 16th-century old-style serif, retaining robust stroke structure and generous letter widths while introducing contemporary cursive inflections and abrupt cuts.
summary: A Plantin-derived text serif by Jungmyung Lee that tempers its old-style structure with exaggerated cursive strokes and abrupt diagonal cuts across eight weights.
description: Rooted in the generous letterwidths and sturdy stroke construction of Plantin, JL Limpet Granite introduces a distinct contemporary character through sharply cut terminals on letters such as K, M, V, W and the double-storey g. The eight-weight range from Thin to Black gives it range across editorial and book settings, while the atypical cutting angles prevent it from reading as a straight revival. A companion italic subfamily, JL Limpet Intertidal, is sold separately.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee Type Foundry describe JL Limpet Granite as a reinterpretation of Plantin that retains its robust stroke structure, generous widths and rich texture, while infusing a touch of femininity and balancing that with bold, contemporary characteristics through exaggerated cursive lines and abruptly cut letterforms.

name: JL Impact Nieuw
classification_notes: A reinterpretation of Impact, one of the most condensed and high-contrast display faces in the Latin canon; Jungmyung Lee's additions of cursive lines, ink-traps and width variations push it firmly into expressive display territory.
summary: A living reinterpretation of Geoffrey Lee's 1965 Impact that grows and mutates across yearly releases, adding cursive strokes, ink-traps and variable widths to the original's rigid compressed form.
description: Jungmyung Lee treats Impact not as a fixed artefact but as an evolving entity, releasing new cuts from 2012 through to 2024 that each reframe the typeface's character. Where the original leaned on strict condensed uniformity, the Nieuw versions introduce flowery cursive lines, ink-traps and width variation that give the family a personality ranging from blunt to buoyant. Each dated subfamily carries its own italics, making the collection a kind of typographic biography rather than a conventional type family.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee Type Foundry describe JL Impact Nieuw as a reinterpreted version of Geoffrey Lee's 1965 Impact, updated with modern values. They point to its cursive lines, ink-traps and changing width variations as the most significant departures from the original's rigid, straight-lined, condensed fixed-width look, and frame the typeface as a person — a growing emotional entity with feelings and thoughts that changes its appearance over time.

name: JL Limpet Intertidal
classification_notes: An italicised companion to JL Limpet Granite, Intertidal is conceived as a condensed, handwriting-inflected italic with unconventional directional axes and irregular letter widths, placing it in display-oriented territory despite its text-capable weight range.
summary: An italicised companion to JL Limpet Granite, designed by Jungmyung Lee, with loosely defined axes and irregular letter widths drawn from the practice of cross-writing.
description: Conceived as a pair to JL Limpet Granite, this face takes its conceptual cue from cross-writing, the historical practice of writing sideways across an already-written page. Characters such as C, e, g, r, s, v, w and y carry unconventional widths that introduce textural friction at the letter level, yet the overall rhythm reads as fluid and coherent. Eight weights from Thin to Black allow it to function both as a textural companion in body settings and as a standalone display voice.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee Type Foundry describe JL Limpet Intertidal as a compatible italicised pair to JL Limpet Granite, sharing a handwriting-like quality while also standing on its own. Inspired by cross-writing, where text is layered sideways over a first pass, the typeface allows multiple directional axes to coexist. Loosely defined axes and unconventional letter widths in characters such as C, e, g, r, s, v, w and y create textural dissonance at the character level, yet as a whole the typeface meanders with tidal fluidity, harmonising its divergent axes.

name: JL Jungka
classification_notes: A grotesk sans-serif positioned deliberately between Akzidenz Grotesk, Helvetica and Univers, with autonomous letterform widths and accentuated circular curves characteristic of the 20th-century European grotesque tradition.
summary: A collaborative grotesque by Karel Martens and Jungmyung Lee, drawn between the formal poles of Akzidenz Grotesk, Helvetica and Univers.
description: Developed between 2013 and 2015, the typeface places itself closer to Akzidenz Grotesk than to Helvetica or Univers, favouring autonomous letterform widths over the uniform grid logic of the latter two. Its accentuated circular curves give it a softer, more rhythmical quality while retaining the legibility expected of a classically informed text sans. Four weights run from Book to Bold, each with a companion italic, covering Latin Extended ranges for Western, Central and South Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee describe JL Jungka as a collaborative typeface made by Karel Martens and Jungmyung Lee, positioned between Akzidenz Grotesk, Helvetica and Univers. They wanted something neither as dry as Univers nor as quirky as Helvetica, landing closer to Akzidenz in its autonomous letter widths and contemporary curves, while adding accentuated circular curves for a soft, friendly rhythm and tuning spacing for modern text legibility.

name: JL Pirelli
classification_notes: A mono-line grotesk revival with horizontal and vertical stroke dominance and no flourishes, placing it squarely in the grotesque sans-serif tradition, though with Art Nouveau-inflected high-waisted capitals that give it a distinctive historical character.
summary: A revival of an anonymous grotesk discovered by Karel Martens, drawn collaboratively with Jungmyung Lee and distinguished by unusually high-waisted capitals that carry echoes of Art Nouveau and Secessionist lettering.
description: The mono-line structure and absence of flourishes give the typeface a concise, upright character typical of early grotesks, but the high crossbars on letters such as E, F and P introduce a period atmosphere that sets it apart from generic revivals. The radical width contrast between uppercase and lowercase adds a contemporary tension to an otherwise historical source. Designed between 2017 and 2018, it is the second collaboration between Martens and Lee following Jungka, available in five weights with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee Type Foundry describe JL Pirelli as a revival of an anonymous grotesk that Karel Martens once came across. Its mostly horizontal and vertical features, mono-line structure and absence of flourishes produce a concise expression, while the distinctive high-waisted capitals — visible in E, F and P — give it the atmosphere of earlier Art Nouveau and Secessionist lettering. The radical width contrast between uppercase and lowercase, they note, speaks of today's time.

name: JL Joseleen
classification_notes: Joseleen features prominent wedge-shaped serifs as its defining structural characteristic, combined with floriated terminals that give it an ornamental, calligraphic quality distinct from transitional or old-style classifications.
summary: A wedge-serif developed from Josse Pyl's earlier 'Permeke' drawing, distinguished by floriated terminals and three sets of stylistic alternates that shift the typeface's character from ornate to austere.
description: Descended from a 2013 drawing by Josse Pyl called Permeke, Joseleen pairs a Roman with an Italic and offers three stylistic alternate sets alongside its default. The default and figure 2 alternates lean into floriate, almost calligraphic details — particularly visible in the K, R, Q and lowercase r — while figure sets 1 and 3 adopt harder, downward-sloping legs for a more direct tone. Latin coverage spans Basic Western European, Central European and South Eastern European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Jung-Lee Type Foundry describe JL Joseleen as a serif typeface developed from 'Permeke', originally drawn by Josse Pyl in 2013 and now paired with an Italic. Its prominent wedge-shaped serifs carry weight and confidence, while floriated details — visible in the K, R, Q and r — lend an elegant edge to the default set and stylistic figure 2. Three sets of stylistic alternates allow the typeface to shift between ornate and more straightforward expression.
